def test_find_in_sorted_last_element():
    # The bug occurs when searching for the last element (7) in the array [3, 4, 5, 6, 7]
    # In the buggy version, when recursing into the upper half, lower bound becomes mid instead of mid + 1
    # This causes infinite recursion because mid stays at the same position
    arr = [3, 4, 5, 6, 7]
    target = 7
    result = find_in_sorted(arr, target)
    # The correct result should be 4 (index of 7 in the array)
    assert result == 4, f"Expected to find 7 at index 4, but got {result}"

def test_find_in_sorted_last_element_infinite_recursion_bug():
    # This test triggers the infinite recursion bug when searching for the last element
    # In the buggy code, binsearch(mid, end) is used instead of binsearch(mid + 1, end)

    target = 7
    # The expected result is index 4 (the last element)

analysis.bug_hypothesis: The bug occurs in the recursive binary search when searching for a target in the upper half of the current range. When x > arr[mid], the function calls binsearch(mid, end) instead of binsearch(mid + 1, end). This causes infinite recursion when the target is at the end of the array because mid never advances past itself.
analysis.trigger_condition: Calling find_in_sorted with a sorted array where the target element is the last element in the array, e.g., find_in_sorted([3, 4, 5, 6, 7], 7)
analysis.expected_vs_actual: Expected: The function should return the index of the target (4 for the example). Actual: The function enters infinite recursion because binsearch(mid, end) keeps calling with the same mid value when the target is in the upper half, never reducing the search space properly.
analysis.suggested_test_strategy: Write a pytest test that calls find_in_sorted([3, 4, 5, 6, 7], 7) and verifies it returns 4 without hanging or hitting recursion limit. Also test with other edge cases like the first element and middle elements.
